plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q1_comment.answer: The nurse and social worker explained that the Plan of Care meeting is a regular check-in to review my lab results, how I am tolerating dialysis, and any goals or concerns I have.
I understand that this is when we decide if any changes are needed to my treatment schedule, my medications related to dialysis, and my diet and fluid limits.
Sometimes the medical terms are a little confusing, but when I ask them to slow down or explain in simpler words, they do, so overall I feel I understand the purpose pretty well.

plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q2_comment.answer: The PoC meeting helped connect my high phosphorus levels with the snacks I was eating at night, and the dietitian gave me specific alternatives like apples and carrot sticks instead of cheese puffs.
We also adjusted my dry weight because I was feeling short of breath after treatment, and since they changed it I feel less cramping and dizziness when I get off the machine.

They coordinated with my primary care doctor about my blood pressure medications so I am not taking certain pills too close to dialysis time, which has reduced the episodes where I felt faint during treatment.

plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q3_comment.answer: The staff asked me directly what my priorities were, and I told them that being able to attend my granddaughter’s school events in the evenings was very important to me, so we kept my Monday/Wednesday/Friday morning schedule.
They listened when I said that the needles were making me very anxious and arranged for the same two techs, Jasmine and Eric, to cannulate me most of the time so I could feel more comfortable.

Sometimes the conversation drifts into medical details between the doctor and nurse that I do not fully follow, but when I speak up they pause and bring me back into the discussion, so I do feel my voice matters overall.

plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q4_comment.answer: I brought up my concern about feeling extremely tired on non-dialysis days and they explained the difference between dialysis fatigue and possible anemia, then ordered labs to check my hemoglobin and iron levels.

I also asked about traveling to visit my sister in Texas, and the social worker walked me through the process of arranging transient dialysis, including giving me a printed checklist and phone numbers.

When I mentioned that I sometimes feel depressed after treatments, the social worker took it seriously, gave me information about counseling options that take my insurance, and encouraged me to call her directly if those feelings get worse.

plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q5_comment.answer: I feel comfortable talking to the nurses and techs about most things, like when my arm feels sore, when I have trouble sticking to my fluid limit, or when I am confused about a lab result.

There are still times when I hesitate to bother the doctor because he seems rushed, but the nurse practitioner has told me several times that it is okay to ask, and she often brings up my questions for me if I am shy.

Overall I know that if something is worrying me—like unusual swelling, trouble sleeping, or side effects from my phosphate binders—I can bring it up at the PoC meeting or even during regular treatments and someone will follow up with me.
